summary: External-review improvement cycle addressing sourcery-ai[bot] feedback on CC-0086. Two changes: (1) the flux-web Chainsaw Step 7 was extracted from tests/e2e/infrastructure/infra-stack-health/chainsaw-test.yaml into a new sibling suite tests/e2e/infrastructure/flux-web-health/chainsaw-test.yaml, gated by a `metadata.labels.overlay: kind` label for CLI selector filtering AND a runtime `kubectl get resourceset flux-web -n flux-system` presence check that exits 0 with SKIP on non-kind clusters; (2) the `renovate-config-validator` (npx-driven) schema check was removed from tests/unit/renovate/flux_web_chart_custommanager_test.sh, with a documented reference that the same renovate.json is already validated by tests/unit/renovate/fluxoperator_custommanager_test.sh (CC-0085) — confirmed: both tests run under `make test-shell` via the `tests/unit/renovate/*_test.sh` glob, and the sibling test exercises the entire file so the flux-web customManager entry is still covered by schema validation.

tests_checklist.item: Tests exist and pass (presumed — worktree is sandboxed; cannot execute chainsaw or kind, reported as test plan)
Edge cases are covered (presence-check short-circuits on non-kind; label selector gives belt-and-braces)
Test coverage for renovate schema validation preserved via sibling test
Catch block dumps post-mortem state (HelmRelease + Deployment + Service describe + podLogs)

code_quality_checklist.item: Follows existing code patterns (chainsaw-e2e-test-suite-structure — deviation from declarative `assert`s to single `script` documented in header)
Clear header comments explain rationale (two gating mechanisms, script-vs-declarative trade-off, centralised validator rationale)
SPDX Apache-2.0 header + Feature: CC-0086 marker present on new chainsaw-test.yaml
Unit test renumbering (Test 2→1, Test 3→2) consistent after removal

security_checklist.item: Presence check uses read-only `kubectl get` — no privilege escalation risk

architecture_checklist.item: Split into dedicated test suite instead of guarding Step 7 inline — keeps infra-stack-health overlay-agnostic
Overlay gate uses both label (declarative) and runtime probe (default-safe) — addresses feedback robustly
Non-duplication of npx-driven validation (centralised in sibling CC-0085 test)

dry_yagni_checklist.item: No duplicated code — header comment explicitly documents where the shared responsibility lives

fail_fast_checklist.item: Presence guard exits early with explicit SKIP message on non-kind
Non-200 HTTP response dumps port-forward log and exits 1

defensive_checklist.item: Trap reaps port-forward PID even on curl failure
Polling loop waits up to 15s for port-forward listener (avoids curl/listener race)
Catch describes resources individually so one missing resource doesn't short-circuit the dump

issues_found.description: Worst-case sequential wall-clock of the flux-web-health script can exceed the 5m exec timeout. Step bounds: `kubectl wait helmrelease/flux-web --timeout=4m` (240s) + `kubectl wait deployment/flux-web --timeout=2m` (120s) + port-forward polling loop (15s) = ~375s. `spec.timeouts.exec: 5m` = 300s. If the HelmRelease is slow to reconcile (e.g. cold OCI chart pull near the 4m bound) AND the Deployment is mid-rollout, chainsaw kills the script mid-execution before the Service/HTTP checks run, producing a confusing timeout failure rather than a useful signal. In the realistic success path, Flux marks HelmRelease Ready=True only after child resources are Available, so the second wait usually returns in <1s — but the budget still doesn't algebraically fit. This matches the `test-script-timeout-must-exceed-worst-case-loop-duration` external-review pattern (WARNING). Fix options: (a) raise `timeouts.exec`/`timeouts.assert` to ~7m, or (b) drop the individual `--timeout=Xm` values to sum under 4m so the script finishes inside the exec budget. Not blocking — the behaviour is already bounded, and in practice the two waits rarely sum near their caps.
issues_found.fix: Raise `spec.timeouts.exec` and the per-step `timeout:` from `5m` to `7m` (or shorten the inner kubectl waits so the sum is <5m with margin) so the worst-case wall-clock stays strictly inside the exec budget.
issues_found.evidence: spec.timeouts.exec: 5m  /  kubectl wait ... helmrelease/flux-web --timeout=4m  /  kubectl wait ... deployment/flux-web --timeout=2m  — 240+120+15 = 375s > 300s exec budget.

suggested_improvements: Consider adding a one-line cross-reference to tests/unit/renovate/fluxoperator_custommanager_test.sh from the sibling test's header so a future contributor removing that CC-0085 test realises it also carries the schema-validation load for CC-0086's flux-web customManager — today the coupling is one-way (flux_web test references flux-operator test, not vice versa).

The `app.kubernetes.io/name=flux-web` selector in the `podLogs` catch step assumes the flux-operator UI chart sets that exact label. If the chart uses a different Helm labelling scheme (e.g. `app=flux-web`), post-mortem pod logs will be empty on failure. Low-cost: verify once against a real rollout and either keep as-is or switch to `name=flux-web` (worktree is sandboxed, can't verify here).
next_steps: Run `make test-shell` on a dev box to confirm the trimmed flux_web_chart_custommanager_test.sh still exits 0 and that fluxoperator_custommanager_test.sh still validates renovate.json (preserves schema coverage for the new file).

Run `chainsaw test --config tests/e2e/chainsaw-config.yaml tests/e2e/infrastructure/` against a fresh kind cluster and confirm both `infra-stack-health` and the new `flux-web-health` pass; then re-run with `--selector 'overlay!=kind'` against a non-kind context (or a kind cluster with the ResourceSet removed) and confirm `flux-web-health` is either filtered out or short-circuits to a PASS via the presence guard.
Consider the MINOR timeout-budget finding above (PF1) — not blocking, but a slow OCI chart pull could surprise CI.
